Venezuelan Youth;Silvana Trevale;FALSE;"The first book by Venezuelan artist Silvana Trevale is now available to pre-order
column2: with a limited 10% discount on the first 100 copies.

column1: Produced between 2016 and 2025
column2: Venezuelan Youth is Silvana Trevale’s contribution to her homeland: a testament that distances itself from portrayals of crises to offer a sensitive and profound view of the complexities of Venezuelan identity as seen through the eyes of its youth.

column1: Yet how do you document a place that many dismiss as a ruin even while it teems with life? For Silvana
column2: the eventual answer was to capture children and adolescents who embody incredible light even during difficult circumstances
column3: so that she might transform the trauma of having left her country into a tribute to the strength of those who still remain. In this way
column4: she’s created a book that reflects the Venezuela that truly exists: “the one I love deeply
column5: a wounded and fearful country
column6: but one that still breathes hope.”

Mawmaw;Anthony Wilson;FALSE;"Mawmaw is an intimate
column2: long-term exploration of West Virginia’s grandfamilies – households where children are being raised by their grandparents. Spanning all four seasons
column3: the work moves beyond the usual narratives around the opioid crisis to examine the many reasons grandparents step into a caregiving role: incarceration
column4: death
column5: unpreparedness

column1: Through photographs made between 2021 and 2026
column2: the book weaves together everyday life — from birthdays and play to exhaustion and resilience — to show both the triumphs and the struggles of these families. At the heart of it all are the grandmothers themselves
column3: who keep these children fed
column4: happy
column5: and most importantly

Close to Home;Laura McCluskey;FALSE;"Shot over a decade on the Isle of Sheppey
column2: where photographic artist Laura McCluskey was raised
column3: Close to Home is a tender interrogation of how ties to family and place may fray and contort yet never break. The project’s narrative heart is 13 Acorn Street
column4: the home of Laura’s paternal grandparents
column5: which was ever central to McCluskey family life.

column2: Laura instinctively began documenting her grandparents
column3: their home and Sheppey itself during each return visit. Thus she was to capture this poignant and candid testament to Jean and Pat’s last years. Her images sensitively portraying mortality’s march as echoed through a residence rich in vital memories. The subdued dilapidation of the once-vibrant 13 Acorn Street until its emptying
column4: underscoring time’s stubborn progress
column5: no matter how much we may long for it to slow.

column1: Place is a further key theme. The places we call home
column2: the tenacious hold they retain over us
column3: which persists despite our relationship to home becoming fraught. For Laura
column4: her love for her grandparents and a desire to mend fractured family ties anchored her to Sheppey. Yet photography was to prove her most powerful healing tool as this instinctual project became a conduit for reconciliation
column5: solace and growth. An enduring act of love to stay Close to Home.

ÈKÓ;Ollie Babajide Tikare;FALSE;"A new photobook by British-Nigerian artist goes in search of the true spirit of Lagos. Èkó captures every day human moments – documenting the calm
column2: the chaos
column3: the dust
column4: colour and a visceral sense of life in this vast Nigerian city.

column1: Èkó is the culmination of Ollie Tikare’s exploration and documentation of Lagos after multiple extended visits to the city. As half of his family’s place of origin and home to several relatives
column2: the city had long been a place Tikare wanted to explore more deeply.

column1: The resulting project speaks of both the beauty and contradictions of bustling urban life. From the fumes of the Lekki–Epe Expressway
column2: quiet contemplations on Tarkwa Bay to the eccentric nightlife on Victoria Island. Tikare’s lens reveals an intimacy with the city that goes beyond observation—to share the spirit of its people and an authentic personal discovery.

column1: The Anthropocene Illusion;Zed Nelson;FALSE;"""While we destroy the natural world around us
column2: we have become masters of a stage-managed
column3: artificial ‘experience’ of nature—a reassuring spectacle
column4: an illusion.""

column1: Over six years
column2: across four continents
column3: Zed Nelson has examined how we humans immerse ourselves in increasingly simulated environments to mask our destructive divorce from the natural world. From theme parks
column4: zoos and natural history museums
column5: to national parks
column7: this work reveals not only a global phenomenon of denial and collective self-delusion
column8: but also a craving for a connection to a world we have turned our back on.

column1: In a fraction of our Earth’s history
column2: we humans have altered our world beyond anything it has experienced in tens of millions of years. Scientists are calling it a new epoch
column3: The Anthropocene – the age of human. We have concentrated in cities and divorced ourselves from the land we once roamed and from other animals. Yet we cannot face the true scale of our loss.

column1: This work reflects on how
column2: at a time of environmental crisis
column3: a consoling version of nature has been packaged as a curated ‘experience’ – an illusionary spectacle designed to obscure and reassure.

ODE;Melissa Schriek;FALSE;"We are excited to launch the stunning and dynamic new book ODE by Melissa Schriek
column2: a vivid exploration into the dynamics of female friendship.

column1: About ODE""One of the primary motivations for starting ODE was to pay homage
column2: quite literally
column3: to sisterhood and female friendship. Throughout my life
column4: I've been inspired by the power and unity of women
column5: and that admiration continues to grow. The bond and connection that I often witness and experience among women hold significant importance to me. It struck me how this bond is frequently misrepresented in popular media as 'toxic'

column1: In response
column2: I set out to create a photographic narrative that would present a different perspective on friendship
column3: while maintaining authenticity. I photographed numerous pairs of best friends
column4: mostly in public space
column5: aiming to explore the essence of their connections through portraiture

column1: Youth Without Age and Life Without Death;Laura Pannack;FALSE;"The inaugural photobook from fine art photographer Laura Pannack. Youth Without Age and Life Without Death marks the first chapter of a long running project in which the artist uses large format photographs
column2: polaroids
column3: drawings and personal notes to grasp at the intagible and explore her connection with time itself. Artist Statement:This project is a response to my need to escape
column4: adventure and roam in reaction to internal pressure I feel that time is moving too fast. It seems like a shared experience that hours
column5: days and weeks pass and we can’t recall them
column6: life continues and time slips away. My adventure began in Romania. The strong sense of timelessness
column7: tradition and untouched scenery mirrored my desire to stop time. I soon stumbled across this local story which perfectly echoes the many questions that I have. This is a photographic exploration of the fabric of time
column9: places and landscapes I encountered in Romania
column10: drawing directly on this famous mythology and a sense of a temporal place to offer a view on the fragility of life.Alternating between reality and fantasy
column11: the photographs respond to a feeling of separation from the modern world and play with the notion of fiction and the present moment. Like a web of nostalgia and resistance
column12: all the signs of temporality come to bear in objects that are charged with tensions of permanence and impermanence. Time elsewhere is relentless but here something of it is left behind.The Book:• 210 x 280mm
column13: hardcover in a golden brown cloth• Image inlaid into the cover and foil embossed title• 112 page silk image section
column14: 60 colour plates• 8 page gloss polaroid section
column15: 12 polaroid plates• 8 page translucent section with pencil drawings• 8 page notes section of artist diaries and notes• First 50 orders receive a foldout broadsheet poster

Padanistan;Tomaso Clavarino
column2: Regular Edition;FALSE;"In the past thirty years the word Padania has become part of everyday life in Italy. It refers to an area in Northern Italy that extends from the Alps to the Adriatic Sea. A territory that exists in the ideas of many
column3: but that geographically
column4: culturally and juridically remains undefined.

column1: Padanistan (using ‘stan’ as an alternative marker for land)
column2: seeks to challenge ownership of this region. Creating a new conceptual space in order to question the existence of Padania and the legitimacy of the right wing politics that created it. The title Padanistan deliberately hijacks the nationalistic rhetoric found within Padanian politics. It questions the notion of place
column3: of boundaries and of defined identities
column4: directly confronting the singular and divisive vision of Italian culture
column5: and the misguided idea of one 'real' Italy.

column1: Padanistan presents a much more nuanced and complex reality
column2: a vision that celebrates a fluid connection with Italy
column3: surrounding countries and the world beyond. The project is the result of a personal view developed as a long road trip along the main national road that connects Torino to Venezia
column4: to discover the places and people living along the way
column5: giving birth to a contemporary reflection on the Northern Italian province.

Scott Rossi;Common Place;FALSE;"Common Place is a visual meditation on Central Park. The project explores the results of intentions set forth by the park’s chief architect
column2: Frederick L. Olmsted
column3: who wanted Central Park to be a democratic space that allows all New Yorkers to escape from the distractions of urban life in Manhattan. Olmsted believed that
column4: by recreating the peacefulness of nature
column5: parks would soothe and restore the human spirit. Never has Olmsted’s vision been more relevant than during this global crisis
column6: which has taken millions of lives while stay-at-home regulations swept across the globe. Whether using the expansive landscape as a place to relax
column8: this project translates these actions photographically. Common Place illustrates the importance of public parks and how during this moment
column9: New Yorkers found what they needed within Central Park.

column1: Caitlin Chescoe;Belief & Truth;FALSE;"'Belief & Truth
column2: Inside the Freemasons' is an exploration of the mysterious brotherhood of Freemasonry by Caitlin Chescoe. Born into a family with three generations of Freemasons
column3: this book goes beyond her close links and reveals insightful surprises about the 300 year old organisation. Having long been labelled an 'old boys club' and 'secret society'
column4: Chescoe shares the individual experiences of both male and female Freemasons focusing on the many pre-conceived ideas about Masonry through a series of portraits
column5: interiors
column7: archives and testimonials.Accompanying the documentary series is an essay written by Camilla Brown which further investigates the history of Freemasonry and its public perception
column10: class and charity as well as a photographic history.

column2: Regular Edition;FALSE;"Cornwall’s last banger and stock car racing track lies in the village of St Day. One by one tracks are disappearing throughout the UK as the subculture succumbs to a slow decline. For 50 years the track at St Day has been kept alive
column3: through love
column4: devotion and community
column5: but it now faces its toughest test. The Last Raceway tells the story of the track as it faces demolition
column6: a tourist attraction planned in its place. Regardless of its physical fate
column7: this book aims to provide a home for the track
column10: in what could be its final season.

Chris Mann;Valley of The Moon;FALSE;"Jordan’s Wadi Rum desert
column2: translated literally as 'Valley of the Moon' in English
column3: is renowned for its red sands
column4: Mars-like topography
column5: and nomadic Bedouin tribes. The series traces the viewer's imagination through a stark depiction of this dream-like world and its ephemera; offering a subjective window into a reality unbound by time or place.

column1: A landscape both unchanged for thousands of years
column2: yet seemingly transient
column3: Valley of the Moon presents a visual dialogue with the desert and its transcendental oscillations between man and nature
column4: light and dark
column5: reality and imagination.

Volcano Songs;Wanda Orme;FALSE;"""What are Volcano Songs? A kind of restless hunger
column2: a flowing
column3: a body joining with other bodies
column4: assembling and reassembling. Intimacy and expanse in equal measure
column5: upheaval and hope.

column1: The photographs and words in Volcano Songs were created on and around the volcano Vesuvius and on Alicudi and Pantelleria
column2: volcanic islands scattered to Italy’s south.

column1: Volcanos are more than one thing. They are both stillness and movement. They have an individuality and a strength yet they arise and fall
column2: they are always in process.

column1: Pressure points
column2: radical upheavals
column3: giving way to destruction and creation
column4: they are a paradox unsolvable and real. Volcanos are apertures
column5: where one world’s truth spills into another.""

column1: Printed and thread sewn in the UK
column2: Volcano Songs has been made using a special semi-pearlescent paper
column3: resulting in a beautiful shimmer that catches the light and brings this ethereal series alive.

Elena Cremona;Postcards from the Past;FALSE;"Postcards from the Past by Elena Cremona is a powerful series of black and white images documenting the landscape of Joshua Tree
column2: California.

column1: In relativity to human experience
column2: landscapes are static things – their changes are slow
column3: their ecosystems cyclical
column4: and any given day is likely to unfold within them much the same as the one prior; it is us that moves through landscapes
column5: shapes and colours them with our emotions

column1: The last time Elena Cremona drove through the vast
column2: alluvial plains of the Mojave Desert
column3: her relationship was ending. Elena and her lover were oscillating at the very edges of one another’s orbits by the time these pictures were taken
column4: and their parting was a sort of fracturing in slow motion across months and geographies. In many ways
column5: the photographs here tell us that. Twenty black and white pictures of the creeping splits in the surfaces of rocks
column6: and Joshua trees most often seen alone
column7: their fronds twisting towards the sky; none of them are of him. She called them postcards for the way she was feeling as she took them
column8: wanting to remain in place geographically
